content.text: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training), introduced by OpenAI in 2021, learns to align visual and linguistic representations in a shared embedding space. Given an image and a text caption, CLIP encodes both into vectors and is trained so that matching pairs are close in the embedding space while mismatched pairs are far apart. The result is a model that can answer "which of these text descriptions best matches this image?" — without ever having seen the specific images or categories during training.

: you describe each category in natural language ("a photo of a cat", "a photo of a dog"), compute similarity scores against those descriptions, and pick the highest-scoring one — all without training a classifier on labelled examples. CLIP matches supervised ResNet-50 on ImageNet zero-shot despite never seeing ImageNet during training.
CLIP consists of two independent encoders that project their respective modalities into a shared

-dimensional embedding space (512 or 768 dimensions depending on variant). The encoders are trained jointly but have no cross-attention between them — their only coupling is through the contrastive loss on their output embeddings.

attrs.alt: CLIP architecture: image encoder (ViT/ResNet) and text encoder (Transformer) produce embeddings that are compared via a 4x4 cosine similarity matrix; diagonal entries are positive pairs (green, maximized), off-diagonal are negative pairs (red, minimized) via the InfoNCE contrastive loss

content.text: Each training step processes a batch of N image-text pairs. The encoders produce N image embeddings and N text embeddings. The
has N positive pairs on the diagonal and N²−N negative pairs off-diagonal. The loss drives the N correct pairs toward similarity 1 and all N²−N incorrect pairs toward 0.

OpenAI evaluated both ResNet (modified with attention pooling) and Vision Transformer (ViT) image encoders. ViT-based variants (ViT-B/32, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14) generally outperform ResNet at the same FLOP budget. The image is resized to 224×224 and split into patches; the CLS token's final-layer representation is projected to the shared embedding dimension via a linear layer.
The text encoder is a 63M-parameter Transformer with a vocabulary of ~49k BPE tokens. Input text is lowercased, tokenised, and padded or truncated to 77 tokens. The final-layer hidden state at the
token position is projected to the shared embedding space. Causal (masked) self-attention is used, so the EOS token attends over the full sequence.
CLIP uses a symmetric version of the InfoNCE (Noise Contrastive Estimation) loss. For a batch of N pairs, let
be L2-normalised image and text embeddings. The scaled cosine similarity is:

is a learned temperature parameter (initialised to 0.07). The loss is the mean cross-entropy across both rows (image→text) and columns (text→image) of this N×N matrix, treating the diagonal as the target class:

With large batch sizes (up to 32,768 in the largest CLIP run), each image sees up to 32,767 negative text examples per step. The sheer quantity of negatives is the driving force behind CLIP's strong alignment.

collected from the internet — a dataset OpenAI calls WebImageText (WIT). The key insight is that the web contains naturally co-occurring text next to images (alt-text, captions, surrounding HTML) at a scale that manual labelling could never match. The dataset is not released publicly, but the community has reproduced a comparable dataset called LAION-400M and the larger LAION-5B.
To classify an image into one of K classes without any training on those classes:
For each class, construct a text prompt: "a photo of a {class_name}".
Encode all K prompts with the text encoder → K text embeddings.
Encode the query image with the image encoder → 1 image embedding.
Compute cosine similarity between the image embedding and each of the K text embeddings.
Apply softmax and take argmax — or return the sorted similarity scores.

# pip install git+https://github.com/openai/CLIP.git torch torchvision
import clip
import torch
from PIL import Image

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-B/32", device=device)

# Load and preprocess an image
image = preprocess(Image.open("cat.jpg")).unsqueeze(0).to(device)

# Define candidate class names — no training data needed
class_names = ["a photo of a cat", "a photo of a dog",
               "a photo of a car", "a photo of a flower"]
texts = clip.tokenize(class_names).to(device)

with torch.no_grad():
    image_features = model.encode_image(image)         # shape: (1, 512)
    text_features = model.encode_text(texts)            # shape: (4, 512)

    # Normalise embeddings to unit sphere
    image_features = image_features / image_features.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    text_features  = text_features  / text_features.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

    # Cosine similarities; scale by model temperature
    logits_per_image = (100.0 * image_features @ text_features.T)
    probs = logits_per_image.softmax(dim=-1).cpu().numpy()

for name, prob in zip(class_names, probs[0]):
    print(f"{name}: {prob:.3f}")
# a photo of a cat: 0.923
# a photo of a dog: 0.052
# ...

Because image and text embeddings live in the same space, you can search an image database with a text query — or search a text corpus with an image query. Pre-compute image embeddings offline, then query in real time:

import os
import torch
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import clip

device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
model, preprocess = clip.load("ViT-B/32", device=device)

# --- Offline: index all images ---
image_paths = [f"images/{f}" for f in os.listdir("images")]
image_embeddings = []
for path in image_paths:
    img = preprocess(Image.open(path)).unsqueeze(0).to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        emb = model.encode_image(img)
        emb = emb / emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    image_embeddings.append(emb)
image_embeddings = torch.cat(image_embeddings)  # (N, 512)

# --- Online: query with text ---
def search(query: str, top_k: int = 5):
    tokens = clip.tokenize([query]).to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        text_emb = model.encode_text(tokens)
        text_emb = text_emb / text_emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    scores = (image_embeddings @ text_emb.T).squeeze()  # (N,)
    top = scores.topk(top_k)
    return [(image_paths[i], scores[i].item()) for i in top.indices]

for path, score in search("a sunny beach with palm trees"):
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {path}")
library provides CLIP with a consistent API alongside every other model. The
class exposes both encoders and the similarity computation:

from transformers import CLIPProcessor, CLIPModel
import torch
from PIL import Image
import requests

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

# Load a remote image
url = "http://images.cocodataset.org/val2017/000000039769.jpg"
image = Image.open(requests.get(url, stream=True).raw)

labels = ["a photo of cats", "a photo of dogs",
          "a photo of a car", "a photo of food"]

inputs = processor(
    text=labels,
    images=image,
    return_tensors="pt",
    padding=True,
)

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)
    # logits_per_image: shape (1, num_labels)
    probs = outputs.logits_per_image.softmax(dim=-1)

for label, prob in zip(labels, probs[0]):
    print(f"{label}: {prob:.3f}")
OpenAI released several CLIP checkpoints trading off speed and accuracy:
Best OpenAI CLIP; used in DALL-E 2

The phrasing of text prompts significantly affects CLIP's classification accuracy. Using the bare class name ("cat") underperforms a descriptive prompt because CLIP was trained on image captions, not keyword lists. The original paper tested 80 handcrafted prompt templates and ensembled their predictions:
— "a photo of a {label}" outperforms the bare label by ~3–5% on ImageNet.
— "a satellite photo of {label}", "an X-ray of {label}", "a closeup photo of {label}" all shift distribution toward the correct visual style.
— average the text embeddings from multiple templates before comparing to the image embedding. This reduces template variance and improves accuracy by 1–3%.
— for visually similar species, adding "a pet" or "wild" to the prompt forces CLIP toward the intended distribution.

# Prompt ensembling: average embeddings across multiple templates
templates = [
    "a photo of a {}",
    "a good photo of a {}",
    "a photo of the {}",
    "a close-up photo of a {}",
    "a blurry photo of a {}",  # handles low-quality inputs
]

def get_text_features_ensemble(class_names, model, device):
    import clip, torch
    all_embeddings = []
    for template in templates:
        prompts = [template.format(c) for c in class_names]
        tokens = clip.tokenize(prompts).to(device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            emb = model.encode_text(tokens)         # (K, 512)
            emb = emb / emb.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
        all_embeddings.append(emb)
    # Average across templates, then re-normalise
    avg = torch.stack(all_embeddings).mean(dim=0)  # (K, 512)
    return avg / avg.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
CLIP as a Foundation for Other Models
CLIP's image encoder became the de-facto vision backbone for many subsequent multimodal systems because its embeddings already carry rich semantic content aligned with language:
— uses CLIP's image encoder to condition a diffusion prior that generates images from text by first generating a CLIP image embedding, then decoding it with a diffusion decoder.
— the text encoder is CLIP's text encoder; it encodes the conditioning prompt whose cross-attention guides the U-Net denoiser.
— use a frozen CLIP image encoder to extract visual features that are projected into an LLM's token space via a lightweight MLP or Q-Former.
— e-commerce and stock-photo platforms use CLIP embeddings to power text-to-image search at scale, replacing keyword-based metadata search.
Despite its broad capabilities, CLIP has well-documented failure modes:
— CLIP cannot reliably answer "are there three dogs?" or "is the cat to the left of the box?" because the embedding compresses scene composition.
— differentiating 200 bird subspecies or distinguishing car models requires domain-specific fine-tuning; zero-shot CLIP struggles with visual subtlety not represented in web captions.
— CLIP can recognise simple logos but is not a reliable OCR system for general text in images.
— the WIT dataset inherits demographic biases, stereotypes, and representation imbalances from internet-scale web crawls.
The community has reproduced and extended CLIP with open weights and larger datasets:
Open weights; matches or exceeds OpenAI CLIP at ViT-H/14 scale
Sigmoid loss instead of softmax; no global normalisation; more efficient large batches
Scaled to 18B params; 80.4% ImageNet zero-shot with EVA-CLIP-18B

While CLIP's zero-shot capability is impressive, fine-tuning on domain-specific data often improves accuracy substantially for narrow tasks. Several adaptation strategies have emerged, each trading generality for specialised performance.

The simplest approach freezes all CLIP weights and trains only a single linear classifier on top of the image embeddings. Because CLIP features are already strongly discriminative, linear probing reaches competitive accuracy with remarkably little labelled data — sometimes just a few shots per class.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

model = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")
processor = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32")

# Freeze all CLIP parameters
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Small linear head on top of visual features (512-d for ViT-B/32)
num_classes = 10
head = nn.Linear(512, num_classes)

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(head.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

def extract_features(images):
    inputs = processor(images=images, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        features = model.get_image_features(**inputs)
    return features / features.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # L2-normalise

# Training loop
for images, labels in DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=64):
    feats = extract_features(images)
    logits = head(feats)
    loss = loss_fn(logits, labels)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
Context Optimisation (CoOp) keeps both encoders frozen but replaces the hand-crafted prompt prefix with a small set of learnable continuous vectors. Instead of writing
Rather than writing "a photo of a {class}" explicitly, CoOp learns an optimal M-token context
directly in the text encoder's token-embedding space. This is far more data-efficient than full fine-tuning, often converging with 1–16 labelled shots.

# Simplified CoOp: learnable prompt prefix, frozen encoders
# Real implementation uses the Dassl library (KaiyangZhou/CoOp)

class CoOpPrompt(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, clip_model, n_ctx=16, n_classes=10):
        super().__init__()
        embed_dim = clip_model.token_embedding.embedding_dim  # 512 for ViT-B/32
        # Learnable context vectors (random init)
        self.ctx = nn.Parameter(torch.empty(n_ctx, embed_dim).normal_(std=0.02))
        self.clip = clip_model
        self.n_classes = n_classes

    def forward(self, class_tokens):
        # class_tokens: (n_classes, embed_dim) from token_embedding
        # Prepend ctx to each class embedding
        ctx = self.ctx.unsqueeze(0).expand(self.n_classes, -1, -1)
        prompts = torch.cat([ctx, class_tokens.unsqueeze(1)], dim=1)
        return prompts  # feed into text encoder's transformer layers
Fully fine-tuning both encoders yields the highest in-domain accuracy but risks catastrophic forgetting — the model loses its general zero-shot capability. Techniques like
(Weight-Space Ensembling for Fine-Tuning): linearly interpolates the weights of the original zero-shot CLIP and the fine-tuned model to preserve generalisation while gaining domain accuracy.
: low-rank adapters inserted into the attention layers of both encoders. Only the adapter weights (~0.5% of parameters) are trained, drastically reducing compute and storage.
: fine-tune only the text encoder when adapting to new label vocabularies. The visual features stay stable, so the model remains usable for image search.
Deploying CLIP in production requires decisions about model size, inference speed, and embedding storage that have direct cost implications.
Start with ViT-B/32; upgrade only if accuracy insufficient
Store as float16 to halve memory; cosine similarity still exact
Pre-encode corpus offline; query only at runtime
FAISS, Qdrant, Weaviate for millions of embeddings
IVF-PQ index: 10-50× compression with <2% recall drop
Export both encoders for platform-agnostic serving
TensorRT INT8 gives 3–4× speedup on NVIDIA hardware

For large-scale image search systems, the typical pattern is: pre-compute and store all image embeddings in a vector database, then at query time encode only the text query and perform approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search. This decoupling means the encoder runs just once per image, regardless of how often it is searched.

CLIP trains two encoders (image ViT/ResNet + text Transformer) jointly by maximising cosine similarity between matching image-text pairs and minimising it for the N²−N mismatches in each batch of N pairs.

Zero-shot classification works by encoding candidate class descriptions as text and selecting the class whose text embedding is most similar to the image embedding — no labelled training data for those classes is required.
Prompt phrasing matters: "a photo of a {class}" consistently outperforms bare class names; ensembling 80 templates adds another 1–3% accuracy on benchmarks.
CLIP's image encoder became the foundation for DALL-E 2, early Stable Diffusion, and LLaVA-family multimodal LLMs; its text encoder directly conditions diffusion model U-Nets in Stable Diffusion 1.x.
Open-source equivalents (OpenCLIP on LAION-2B, SigLIP, EVA-CLIP-18B) match or surpass OpenAI CLIP at larger scale; SigLIP replaces the softmax loss with sigmoid for more training efficiency.
